coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent turn-off/on method based on carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the sensitive determination of Pb2+ and pyrophosphate in an aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qin
Zhang, Shengrui
Ge, Hongguang
Tian, Guanghui
Cao, Nini
Li, Yaqin
coredata.dc:description: Unmodified carbon dots (CDs) were demonstrated as novel and environmentally friendly fluorescent probes for sensing Pb2+ and pyrophosphate (PPi) in an aqueous solution. The prepared CDs had an average diameter of 50nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the CD solution was sensitive to the pH and exhibited a yellow-green color under UV light (365nm). The approach relied on the fact that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was effectively quenched by Pb2+ (turn-off), and the resulting CD-Pb2+ system was observed to be sensitive to PPi. Due to the ability of PPi to coordinate with Pb2+, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was effectively protected and reversibly restored by the addition of PPi (turn-on). Good linearity for detecting Pb2+ was attained over the concentration range from 5.0×10−7 to 1.1×10−5 mol/L. The binding constant between Pb2+ and CDs was 5.0×104 L/mol with a detection limit of 2.0×10−9 mol/L. The optimized conditions for detecting PPi, such as the incubation time, pH of the CD solution and CD concentration, were 3.0min, and 7.0 and 0.07mg/mL, respectively. Under the optimized conditions, the concentrations of PPi ranging from 1.0×10−5 to 1.7×10−3 mol/L exhibited a linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.9948. The detection limit for PPi was 5.0×10−7 mol/L. The fluorescent turn-off/on method offered good selectivity for detecting PPi in the presence of 18 different types of ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pyrophosphate
Pb2+
Fluorescent probe
Turn-off/on

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quenching effect of Pb2+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs
Detection of PPi in aqueous solutions
Effects of coexisting ions on detecting Pb2+ or PPi
Quenching effect of Pb2+ on CD fluorescence intensity
Fluorescence restoration of the CD-Pb2+ system by PPi
Effect of the pH and CD concentrations on the recovery efficiency
Determination of PPi in aqueous solutions
Determination of the PPi concentration in urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of Pb2+ and PPi detection using CDs.
(A) SEM image of the CDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of (a) kerosene soot and (b) CDs. (C) Emission and excitation spectra of the CDs. (D) pH effects on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs.

Photograph of the CD solution excited by daylight (a) and a 365-nm UV lamp (b). Fluorescent images on commercially available paper of characters composed from water (c) and commercially available yellow-green fluorescent ink under UV light (365nm).
The optimization of the reaction time for Pb2+ detection. [CD]=0.1mg/mL, [Pb2+]=3.0×10−6 mol/L; and Tris buffer (pH 7.0).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of Pb2+. (a) CD, 0.10mg/mL; (b–k) CD-Pb2+, the Pb2+ concentrations of which were 5.0×10−7, 2.0×10−6, 3.0×10−6, 4.0×10−6, 5.0×10−6, 6.0×10−6, 7.0×10−6, 8.0×10−6, 9.0×10−6, and 1.1×10−5 mol/L, respectively. (B) Stern–Volmer plots of CD fluorescence-intensity quenching by Pb2+. [CD]=0.10mg/mL and [Pb2+]=5.0×10−7 −1.1×10−5 mol/L.
Emission spectra of the CDs (a), CDs mixed with Pb2+ (b) and CD-Pb2+ system mixed with PPi (c). [CD]=0.1mg/mL; [Pb2+]=3.0×10−4 mol/L; and [PPi]=1.2×10−3 mol/L.

(A, B) The optimization of the incubation time for PPi detection. [CD]=0.1mg/mL; [Pb2+]=3.0×10−4 mol/L; [PPi]=1.2×10−3 mol/L; and Tris buffer (pH 7.0). (C) The optimization of pH: [CD]=0.1mg/mL, [Pb2+]=3.0×10−4 mol/L, and [PPi]=1.2×10−3 mol/L. (D) The optimization of the CD concentrations: [Pb2+]=3.0×10−4 mol/L, [PPi]=1.2×10−3 mol/L; and Tris buffer (pH 7.0).

(A) Fluorescence response of the CD-Pb2+ system in the presence of an increasing concentration of PPi. (a–j) The PPi concentrations were 0, 1.0×10−5, 8.0×10−5, 2.0×10−4, 4.0×10−4, 6.0×10−4, 8.0×10−4, 1.0×10−3, 1.2×10−3 and 1.7×10−3 mol/L, respectively. (B) A linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of PPi. [CD]=0.07mg/mL; [Pb2+]=3.0×10−4 mol/L; [PPi]=1.0×10−5 to 1.7×10−3 mol/L; and Tris buffer (pH 7.40).
Elemental analysis of the raw kerosene soot and CDs.
Effects of coexisting ions on determination of Pb2+.
The effects of coexisting ions on the determination of PPi using CDs-Pb2+ system.
The effects of different interfering agents on determinating PPi in urine sample.
Results for the determination of PPi concentration in human urine.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent turn-off/on method based on carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the sensitive determination of Pb2+ and pyrophosphate in an aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Unmodified carbon dots (CDs) were demonstrated as novel and environmentally friendly fluorescent probes for sensing Pb2+ and pyrophosphate (PPi) in an aqueous solution. The prepared CDs had an average diameter of 50nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the CD solution was sensitive to the pH and exhibited a yellow-green color under UV light (365nm). The approach relied on the fact that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was effectively quenched by Pb2+ (turn-off), and the resulting CD-Pb2+ system was observed to be sensitive to PPi. Due to the ability of PPi to coordinate with Pb2+, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was effectively protected and reversibly restored by the addition of PPi (turn-on). Good linearity for detecting Pb2+ was attained over the concentration range from 5.0×10−7 to 1.1×10−5 mol/L. The binding constant between Pb2+ and CDs was 5.0×104 L/mol with a detection limit of 2.0×10−9 mol/L. The optimized conditions for detecting PPi, such as the incubation time, pH of the CD solution and CD concentration, were 3.0min, and 7.0 and 0.07mg/mL, respectively. Under the optimized conditions, the concentrations of PPi ranging from 1.0×10−5 to 1.7×10−3 mol/L exhibited a linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity with a correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.9948. The detection limit for PPi was 5.0×10−7 mol/L. The fluorescent turn-off/on method offered good selectivity for detecting PPi in the presence of 18 different types of ions.

ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of adverse effects in the bone, brain, kidney, liver, central nervous system, cardiovascular system and immune system have been attributed to Pb2+ poisoning [1,2]. Moreover, when Pb2+ poisoning is defined as blood levels higher than 5μmol/L, it causes adverse environmental impacts and human-health-related problems, particularly for children, such as premature birth, sore muscles, anemia, headaches, fatigue and irritation [3]. Therefore, developing low-complexity sensing approaches with high affinities for detecting Pb2+ remains an active research area. In the past decade, a range of instrumental techniques, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductive coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, have been widely used [4–6]. However, these methods generally require long measuring periods, high costs and skillful operation, and fluorescence-based chemosensors have therefore received significant attention because of their cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, simple sample preparation, real-time monitoring with fast response times, and the lack of necessity of a reference solution [7,8].

On the other hand, anions are ubiquitous throughout biological systems and play a fundamental role in a wide range of chemical and biological processes [9]. As an anion, pyrophosphate (diphosphate, P2O4 −7 or PPi) is released upon hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate to adenosine monophosphate under cellular conditions [10]. It is a part of the biological energy cycle and DNA synthesis, and it is also an inhibitor of several crystallization reactions [11,12]. Therefore, numerous efforts have been devoted to the development of abiotic reporters for PPi, and these works have become important in medicine, biology, the environment and nutriology [13]. Among the varieties of methods, fluorescent chemosensors have attracted considerable attention because of their high selectivity, sensitivity and simplicity [14]. However, among the numerous PPi receptors reported to date, only a few examples exist of effective fluorescent sensors in 100% aqueous solutions for PPi. The majority of reports have concentrated on their behavior in nonaqueous solvents, and most of these methods are based on the synthesis of organic small-molecule probes, which are complicated and time-consuming, and require organic solvents as the reaction media [15].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have received increased interest, owing to their favorable properties, such as their high surface area, special electronic, optical and thermal characteristics, low toxicity and biocompatibility [16,17]. They can be synthesized using a simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly combustion/thermal approach [18,19], and the CDs obtained by this method are functionalized with carboxylic/carbonyl moieties on the particle surfaces, to which metal ions can coordinate [20–22]. Until now, scientific papers about metal ions and biological-molecule sensing based on functionalized CDs have increased. Esteves da Silva et al. presented a novel biocompatible nanosensor for measuring Hg2+ [16]; Qu et al. used CDs as novel and environmentally friendly fluorescent probes for sensing Hg2+ [23]; and Zhang et al. performed a CD-based fluorescence turn-on method for detecting DNA [24]. Although these CD-based materials hold great promise in nanotechnology and nanomedicine, significant research is still necessary to explore the full potential of these nanomaterials in the development of advanced smart sensors.

Compared with the detection of Pb2+, it is difficult to selectively recognize PPi in water because the electrostatic binding interactions of anions are less effective than they are for smaller cations. Moreover, anions are very sensitive to pH values in aqueous systems, and detecting anions in aqueous solutions requires a much stronger affinity than that of cations in water [25,26]. In the present work, we explored a fluorescent turn-off/on method using CDs as a type of water-soluble fluorescent probe (schematic diagram is depicted in Fig. 1 ) to realize a simple, rapid and low-cost analysis of Pb2+ and PPi. Compared to a reference, the metal ions can bind with the functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which influences their surface states and fluorescence intensity [21]. Because of this binding interaction, Pb2+ was added into the CD solution, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched significantly due to charge-transfer via the inner-filter effect. Because of the strong coordination of PPi with Pb2+, when the PPi was added, a competitive interaction existed between Pb2+, PPi and the CDs, which disturbed the interaction between Pb2+ and the CDs. It was then possible to remove the Pb2+ from the surface of the CDs, and thus the fluorescence intensity of the system could be recovered dramatically. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of CDs could be quenched in the presence of Pb2+ and recovered by the addition of PPi, and accordingly, a highly sensitive fluorescence sensor was fabricated for detecting Pb2+ and PPi by taking advantage of the observed fluorescence change. The effects of the experimental conditions, such as the incubation time, pH of the CD solution, CD concentration and interference of ions, were systematically investigated.

Kerosene from a local market was used in this work. Tris(tris(hydroxymethyl)-amino-methane) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Standard stock solutions of anions were prepared by dissolving spectral pure grade chemical reagents in deionized double-distilled water with the addition of hydrochloric acid (The First Reagent Factory, Shanghai, China) and further diluted daily prior to use. A Tris (0.2mol/L)–HCl (0.1mol/L) buffer solution was used to maintain the pH of the solutions at 7.0. Dialysis bags (MW cutoff=8kDa) were purchased from Millipore Corporation (Billerica, USA). Deionized double-distilled water was used throughout the experiment. The raw kerosene soot was collected by placing a glass plate on top of a smoldering kerosene lamp. All materials were analytical grade and spectral purity. The deionized double-distilled water was used throughout.

The microstructures of the CDs were examined using a JSM-6701F scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer within the range of 500–4000cm−1. A Vario EL elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany) was used for elemental analysis of the kerosene soot and CDs. All fluorescence spectra were recorded using an F97Pro spectrofluorophotometer (LengGuang Industrial Co., Ltd. of Shanghai, China), and the excitation and emission bandwidths were both 10nm. The pH adjustments were monitored using a pHS-10C digital pH meter (Xiaoshan Instrument Factory, China). The CD solutions were centrifuged using a TDL-60B centrifuge (Shanghai Anting Science Apparatus Factory, China). The photograph and fluorescent images of the CD solutions were performed using a ZF1 UV analyzer.

First, 1.20g of kerosene soot was added into 240mL HNO3 (5mol/L) and refluxed at 125°C for 72h. The black suspension was centrifuged at 4000rpm for 30min, and the brownish-yellow supernatant was collected and neutralized using NaOH. Next, it was dialyzed against deionized double-distilled water through a dialysis bag for 3 days. Finally, the brownish-yellow solution containing the CDs was collected and saved for analysis. A stock solution of the CDs at a concentration of 1.0mg/mL was prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount in deionized double-distilled water, and it was stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of Pb2+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of CDs was diluted to 0.10mg/mL with a Tris buffer solution (pH 7.0) (In acidic and alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was weak, and in alkaline conditions, Pb2+ would combine with OH− and precipitate, which was not conducive to the formation of CDs-Pb2+ aggregates, and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was not well quenched. Therefore, a partially neutral environment was chosen.). Then, 1.0mL CDs (0.10mg/mL) was titrated by adding the Pb2+ ion solution at concentrations ranging from 5.0×10−7 to 1.1×10−5 mol/L. These solutions were allowed to stand for 3min to equilibrate, and the intensity was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PPi in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: First, 1.0mL CD solution (0.10mg/mL) was titrated by adding 10μL 3×10−2 mol/L Pb2+ working solution (after many experiments, the fluorescence quenching of the CDs tended to be stable when the concentration of Pb2+ was 3.0×10−4 mol/L in the system, which was not shown in the paper), and then the solution was titrated by successive additions of a 1.0×10−3 mol/L PPi solution with a trace syringe. These solutions were allowed to stand for 3min to equilibrate, and the intensity was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of coexisting ions on detecting Pb2+ or PPi
ce:section.ce:para: A series of sample solutions containing Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+ and Fe3+ were added to 1.0mL each of CD solution (0.10mg/mL). Then, these solutions were titrated by adding 10μL 3.0×10−4 mol/L Pb2+ solution with a trace syringe. These solutions were allowed to stand for 3min to equilibrate, and the intensity was recorded.

A series of sample solutions containing F−, S2O3 2−, IO3 −, S2O8 2−, Br−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, ClO3 −, SiO3 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2−, SCN−, BrO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 −, C2O4 −, and PO4 3− ions were added to 1.0mL each of CD-Pb2+ solutions. Then, these solutions were titrated by adding 10μL 1×10−3 mol/L PPi solution with a trace syringe. These solutions were allowed to stand for 3min to equilibrate, and the intensity was recorded.

First, 100mL of a urine sample was mixed with 0.1mL of HCl (6mol/L), and then the mixture was purified using a chromatographic column with an inner diameter of 8mm containing 0.25g of activated carbon [27].

Next, 2.0mL of a solution of 1.6mL of perchloric acid (1mol/L) and 0.4mL of ammonium molybdate (0.08mol/L) was added into 7.0mL of the decolorized urine sample, and after the solution had been thoroughly mixed, 0.8mL of 0.1mol/L triethylamine hydrochloride (pH 5.0) was added. The mixture was centrifuged at 4000rpm for 15min, and the clear supernatant solution was poured into another tube and centrifuged again at 12,000rpm for 20min [28].

To precipitate all of the Mg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ existing in the urine sample, a small quantity of a suitable concentration of NaOH was introduced into the sample, and the resulting colloidal precipitate was thus formed. (It is important that no precipitate be carried over into the next step. If necessary, the supernatant should be centrifuged once more.) After centrifugation at 4000rpm for 15min, the pH of the clear supernatant was adjusted to 7.4 with HCl. Then, 70μL of CD stock solutions (1.0mg/mL), 10μL of Pb2+ solutions (3.0×10−2 mol/L), and 500μL of the treated urine sample were mixed and diluted to 1mL with a Tris buffer solution (pH 7.0). The fluorescence spectra of the resultant mixtures were recorded.

The kerosene soot was black in color and completely insoluble in HNO3 (5mol/L) even after ultrasonication, which was attributed to their large size and hydrophobic nature. When this soot was refluxed with HNO3 (5mol/L), a light-brown supernatant solution was obtained, and the brownish-yellow supernatant indicated that the CDs became small and water-soluble during the refluxing process. To identify the size and morphology of the CDs, the size dispersion and formation of the resulting nanoparticles was evaluated by SEM [29,30]. The solution of CDs was deposited onto a Cu substrate. As shown in Fig. 2A, it can be observed that the size dispersion of the particles was relatively small, with the majority of the particles falling within the range of 37.5 to 56.2nm in diameter, which was similar to the results of Zhang et al. [31].

An FT-IR spectrum was acquired to determine the surface state of the CDs, as shown in Fig. 2B. The peak centered at 3371.56cm−1 may be assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH bonds, whereas the peak at 1423.76 was attributed to the bending vibrations of CH bonds. The stretching vibrations of CO bonds (1823.31, 1789.62 and 1664.83cm−1) were detected, and two peaks at 2806.24 and 2733.19cm−1 can be found, which most likely arose from the CH bending vibrations of the carboxyl functional groups. On the basis of these measurements, the prepared CDs were surrounded by COOH and OH groups. These surface groups enable the prepared CDs to exhibit good water solubility and could coordinate with other metal ions.

The purified, solid-state CDs were obtained by drying the CD solution. Table 1 showed that the purified CDs possessed quite different chemical compositions from that of the raw diesel soot. The oxygen and nitrogen contents of CDs were both much higher than those of the diesel soot. These results suggested that nitrogen and oxygen were incorporated into the raw diesel soot particles via nitric acid oxidation, which produced their water solubility.

The excitation and emission spectra of the CDs in water were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2C, a very well-defined excitation peak could be observed at 471nm, and an emission peak at 530nm. It is well known that the optical properties of CDs might arise from particle surface states and are sensitive to the pH of the solution [32]. Thus, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was studied. As shown in Fig. 2D, the pH of the solutions was investigated from pH 1 to 11, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs was sensitive to the pH. The fluorescence intensity reached its maximal value at pH 7.0. This phenomenon may be attributed to the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of CDs in acidic and alkali media.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3 , the color of the CD solution synthesized via nitric acid oxidation was yellow and exhibited a yellow-green color under UV light (365nm). In addition, on commercially available paper, these characters showed no fluorescence, and under UV light (365nm), the yellow-green fluorescent characters THANK YOU were observed. These phenomena indicated that the prepared CDs were synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching effect of Pb2+ on CD fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the response rate between CDs and Pb2+, when 10μL of a 3.0×10−4 mol/L Pb2+ solution was added into a 0.1mg/mL CD solution, the time-dependent fluorescence changes were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 , fluorescence quenching of CDs was observed within 0.5min of the contact time, which meant that the response rate was rapid and was highly desirable for ion detection. The fluorescence quenching became slow until reaching a steady state at 2min. A further increase in time did not lead to any further detectable quenching of the fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, 2min was selected as the optimal reaction time for Pb2+ detection to achieve a low detection limit.

As shown in Fig. 5A, when the Pb2+ was added into the 0.1mg/mL CD solution, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched because of the strong coordination ability between Pb2+ and the carboxylic moieties on the CD surface [33]. The interaction would undermine the stability of the luminescent properties of surface functional groups, leading to the aggregation of CDs and causing fluorescence quenching. The quenching of the fluorescence intensity by Pb2+ could be described using the Stern–Volmer equation [34,35]:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[
Pb
2 +
]
where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity without Pb2+, F is the fluorescence intensity observed in the presence of the Pb2+ and K SV is the Stern–Volmer (conditional stability) constant. A typical Stern–Volmer plot was shown in Fig. 5B, and a calibration curve of F 0/F =0.9977+5.0×104[Pb2+] was obtained. Good linearity between the F 0/F (Y) and the Pb2+ concentrations (X) was attained over the concentration range from 5.0×10−7 to 1.1×10−5 mol/L. The binding constant of Pb2+ with CDs was 5.0×104 L/mol, with a detection limit of 2.0×10−9 mol/L. Furthermore, the precision expressed as the relative standard deviation (RSD), obtained from a series of 11 standard samples each containing 3.0×10−6 mol/L of Pb2+, was 2.43%.

Many research groups have performed notable works related to detecting Pb2+. Zheng's group developed an ionic liquid-supported CeO2 nanoparticle/carbon nanotube composite-enhanced electrochemical DNA-based sensor for the detection of Pb2+, for which the detection limit was 5.0×10−9 mol/L [36]; Afkhami's group presented a Pb2+ potentiometric sensor using a modified core/shell SiO2/Fe3O4 nanostructure, which had a detection limit of 9.5×10−10 mol/L [37]; and Chen's group fabricated an ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical sensor for Pb2+ based on the allosteric transition of G-Quadruplex DNAzyme, for which the detection limit was 1.0×10−8 mol/L [38]. Compared to previously reported results, the sensitivity of the proposed method for determining Pb2+ was as good as that of the previously reported methods, indicating its high detection sensitivity.

In addition to the sensitivity requirement, a high specificity is crucial for Pb2+ detection. To evaluate the specificity of CDs for a concentration of Pb2+ of 3.0×10−6 mol/L, we measured the fluorescence changes in the presence of various metal ions, including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+ and Fe3+, under the same conditions. The corresponding changes in the fluorescence intensity and RSD values were given in Table 2 . Certain metal ions (Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+) only caused a negligible fluorescence change (the change was less than 7%), whereas other metal ions (Fe2+, Ni2+ and Fe3+) had significant interference for the determination of Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence restoration of the CD-Pb2+ system by PPi
ce:section.ce:para: After being quenched by Pb2+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was restored in the presence of PPi (as shown in Fig. 6 ). As mentioned above, the addition of PPi created a second equilibrium with Pb2+, which perturbed the interaction of the CD-Pb2+ and removed Pb2+ from the surface of CDs. Thus, the CD-Pb2+ system could be used for the quantitative determination of PPi. The experimental conditions, including the incubation times, pH values of CD solutions and concentrations of CDs, were studied in subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence restoration of the CD-Pb2+ system induced by PPi was sensitive to the incubation time. As shown in Fig. 7A, the fluorescence-recovery value of CDs was stable after 2min and changed slightly after 8min (where F 0 and F 1 were the fluorescence intensities of the CD-Pb2+ mixtures in the absence and presence of PPi, respectively). With increased incubation time, the fluorescence-recovery value decreased gradually, which was due to the electrostatic-bonding ability between CDs and Pb2+ because the continuous process of electrostatic attraction may lower the complexation of Pb2+ and PPi (shown in Fig. 7B). When the electrostatic bonding effects of the CDs, Pb2+ and PPi reached equilibrium, the fluorescence-recovery value of the CDs tended to be constant. Based on the above results, the incubation time was chosen as 3min in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the pH and CD concentrations on the recovery efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: After 100μL of CD stock solutions (1.0mg/mL), 10μL of Pb2+ solutions (3.0×10−2 mol/L), and 40μL stock solutions of PPi (3.0×10−3 mol/L) were mixed and diluted to 1mL with a Tris buffer solution (pH 7.0), the fluorescence spectra were recorded. Fig. 7C showed that as the pH value increased, the PPi-induced fluorescence restoration increased gradually. When the pH value was 7.0, the restoration reached its maximal value. Additionally, when the pH value was above 7.0, Pb2+ was easy to hydrolyze, and the repeatability of the fluorescence-intensity measurements was very poor at pH values below 7.0 (for three replicate measurements, the RSD was 25.62%). Considering the sensitivity and the repeatability, 7.0 was chosen as the optimal pH value. As shown in Fig. 7D, various concentrations of CDs (ranging from 0.01 to 0.1mg/mL) were mixed with Pb2+ and PPi. The fluorescence restoration of the CD-Pb2+ system increased as the CD concentration increased and reached its maximal value at 0.07mg/mL. Thus, a CD concentration of 0.07mg/mL was selected as the optimal value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PPi in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence recovery values of CD-Pb2+ in the presence of various concentrations of PPi were recorded. As shown in Fig. 8A, upon the introduction of PPi, a significant increase in the emission intensity was observed. Because the increasing fluorescence intensity of the CD-Pb2+ system caused by the addition of PPi was proportional to the PPi concentration, a calibration curve of F 1 − F 0 =21.52+22.43×104[PPi] (R 2 =0.9948) was obtained, allowing the quantification of PPi in the range of 1.0×10−5 to 1.7×10−3 mol/L (Fig. 8B) (where F 0 and F 1 express the same meaning as that in Fig. 7) [39]. The detection limit of the fluorescence changes calculated on the basis of 3σ/K was as low as 5.0×10−7 mol/L. Pitchumani's group used per-6-ammonium-β-cyclodextrin/p-nitrophenol complex as a colorimetric sensor for detecting PPi in water, with a detection limit of 5.0×10−5 mol/L [40]; and Yen's group developed a novel, highly selectively colorimetric and fluorescent chemosensor for PPi in a CH3CN/DMSO/H2O (85/10/5, v/v/v) solution, for which the detection limit of the fluorescence changes calculated on the basis of 3σ/K was 3.0×10−7 mol/L [41]. The sensitivity of this proposed method for detecting PPi was as good as or better than those of the previously reported methods, indicating its high detection sensitivity. The precision expressed as the RSD, obtained from a series of 11 standard samples each containing 6.0×10−4 mol/L of PPi, was 1.35%.

The selectivity of this CD-Pb2+ sensing system was estimated. When the concentration of PPi was 1.0×10−5 mol/L, the effects of 18 other types of anions on the fluorescence response of the CD-Pb2+ system were investigated. The effects of an excess that was 200-fold or greater for F−, S2O3 2−, IO3 − and S2O8 2−; 80-fold for Br−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, ClO3 −, SiO3 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2−, SCN− and BrO3 −; 20-fold for H2PO4 −, HPO4 − and C2O4 −; and fourfold for PO4 3− ions on the recovery of PPi for the CD-Pb2+ composite fluorescence probe were investigated. As shown in Table 3 , these anions did not interfere with the CD-Pb2+ sensing system. This result demonstrated that the chemosensing ensemble had a high selectivity for the determination of PPi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the PPi concentration in urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: The urinary level of PPi represents a major inhibitor of urinary crystallization and plays an important role in the formation of calcigerous stones. Thus, the content of PPi in human urine was assessed using the proposed approach. The selectivity of the sensor for PPi in the presence of the interfering species in a urine sample was first investigated by the addition of various amounts of the interfering agent to the samples containing 1.0×10−5 mol/L PPi. The results were summarized in Table 4 . Most substances existing in the urine sample, such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, urea, uric acid, and BSA, showed no remarkable interference in the determination of the level of PPi, even at high concentrations. Finally, the PPi concentration in a human urine sample was measured using the calibration curve. The PPi concentration determined in the urine samples was 5.4×10−5 mol/L, which was consistent with the values obtained by another method [42]. The quantitative results and the recoveries of the proposed method were determined by adding various amounts of PPi into the sample, and the results were listed in Table 5 .

A new type of CD-based sensor for the highly sensitive detection of Pb2+ and PPi was presented. Pb2+ was able to quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the CD-Pb2+ system can effectively be recovered by the addition of PPi because of its ability to remove Pb2+ from the surface of CDs. There were three distinct improvements in this assay. First, the approach presented herein was simple and efficient. It presented a convenient “mix-and-detect” protocol for the rapid detection of Pb2+ and PPi, and this method can be easily accomplished with one-step rapid operation. Second, this fluorescence sensor exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity toward PPi versus 18 other types of anions. In addition, this sensing platform was able to effectively distinguish PPi from phosphate. Third, no further chemical modification of CDs was required, and the novel strategy eliminated the necessity for organic dyes and an organic solvent, which made the process more environmentally friendly. The novel approach presented here may pave the way for the construction of highly selective and sensitive nanoprobes for anions in biological or environmental samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Educational Scientific Research Project of Shaanxi Province of China (14JK1145), the Startup Project of Shaanxi University of Technology (SLGQD13-3), the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21373132) and the Society Progress Project of Shaanxi Province of China (2012K19-03-03).

Fluorescence relay enhancement sequential recognition of Cu2+ and CN− by a new quinazoline derivative
Spiropyran-based fluorescent anion probe and its application for urinary pyrophosphate detection

Study on the fluorescence carbon nanoparticles
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot

Determination of pyrophosphate in renal calculi and urine by means of an enzymatic method

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qin Wang is a lecturer in the School of Chemistry and Environmental Science at Shaanxi University of Technology (PR China). She received her PhD degree from Lanzhou University in 2012. Her research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and sensing materials for optical sensors.

Shengrui Zhang is an assistant experimentalist in the School of Chemistry and Environmental Science at Shaanxi University of Technology (PR China). He received his master's degree from Lanzhou University in 2012. His current research interest involves the design and synthesis of fluorescent probes.

Hongguang Ge is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Environmental Science at Shaanxi University of Technology (PR China). He received his PhD degree from Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology in 2004. His main research fields include the design and synthesis of coordination compounds and fluorescent probes.

Guanghui Tian is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Environmental Science at Shaanxi University of Technology (PR China). He received his bachelor's and master's degree from Shaanxi Normal University in 1995 and 2004, respectively. He is a postgraduate tutor in botany. His research interests include the separation and analysis of active ingredients in medicinal plants, research on, and development of, natural products, and the design and synthesis of fluorescent chemosensors.

Nini Cao is a student in the School of Chemistry and Environmental Science at Shaanxi University of Technology (PR China), under the supervision of lecturer Qin Wang. Her research interests include the design and synthesis of carbon dot-based sensors for metal ions.

Yaqin Li is a student in the School of Chemistry and Environmental Science at Shaanxi University of Technology (PR China), under the supervision of lecturer Qin Wang. Her research interests include the design and synthesis of carbon dot-based sensors for anions.
